item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There could be a bit more done with this scip like filling in level 33 as mentioned above and possibly clarifying how the player actually interacts with their surroundings while playing. That said it's not a bad scip just not quite good enough to give a +1.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced and is assumed to be non-compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this +1

I know it's a little necro but a test I would like to see is: make a copy of the game, have someone start with the copy and then destroy the copied cartridge. Would be interesting to see if it frees them from it or what.

Also, along somewhat similar lines, can someone play the original and someone else play the copy at the same time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember a creepypasta that was this almost verbatim. I forgot the name of it but this was exactly what the game did in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… I wonder what SCP that level 18-1 resembled…
Reads "easier for two"
O H H H

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can actually see this as a real game for VR Headsets…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Video game creepypastas are my guilty pleasure. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it would have been more interesting if it didn't kill you. We have a lot of things that kill you. How about you make a horrifying experience that left you alive? Y'know, like a game? It can still mess with your head.

…But I still +1'd, so whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Oh hey, look, a video game skip with an easy-to-make player select screen as its only image. Downv-… oh. Okay. That makes sense, I guess." Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://prntscr.com/57ml1e
I don't think that's supposed to be there…

item.title: Re: Loving it!
item.content:encoded: Or game genie!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Test Log 1315-1 seems to omit level 30, is this intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what are you talking about, the image formatting is scantacular

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, nobody cares about the image's format?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also agree that level 33 (and maybe more levels) should be written out. Just imagine if someone from one of the U.S's special forces played this with a partner, I bet they'd get farther than level 33. But the foundation doesn't use America's golden boys (and girls) as guinea pigs.

Just a suggestion, but +1!

item.title: Loving it!
item.content:encoded:
I agree that level 33 should be written out, even if details are expunged. otherwise it seems to fall a little flat as is.

Also, has researcher tried the konami code? It might help with extra lives and all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For once, a video game I do not want to play…

Ever….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading this I was actually thinking the same thing.
Plug that thing into a GameGenie!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You should still write up level33. It seems a bit of a bummer that you never came up with a level33, and if you have, why u not telling? Perhaps it was some epic final boss battle against Dr. Goul on an epic platform suspended over a pit of boiling lava.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like this, and I'm surprised it's decently rated. Good writing doesn't make up for a boring take on an overdone idea. And this is a thoroughly boring take to me. (Though I suppose the degree to which I don't like it is partly just disappointment.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want an actual video game based on this. It'd be so cool…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doctor Wondertainment does not approve of Professor Ghoul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who wants to try hooking the thing up with a GameGenie? That might get an interesting result.

Also, what really makes this thing scary is how retro-gaming collectors work. A rare game that there is only one known copy of? Let's make a repo of it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually find it interesting that you can copy it onto a computer and put that copy onto a cartridge, and the cartridge will work, but that same info on the computer itself will not work.

Haha, I just figured out a good reason why a ROM wouldn't work. You have save-states in emulators. That's cheating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good lord, I just had this idea in my head, and cringed and laughed at the same time.

Test log
some date
A safety cage was provided for D-class test subject. Subject entered cage when one adult Kodiak bear appeared during Level 2. Subject killed bear with shotgun, but refused to leave the cage. Approx. five minutes later, two Kodiak bears appeared inside the cage; due to the cramped and confined area, D-class was effectively surrounded and mauled by the bears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this is getting to be one of my pet peeves. Just redacting the last item in a list does not work as well as people think it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, a video game SCP that works! That test log is really good, slowly ramping up first the danger and then the horror.

My big question is, what are the players doing this whole time? Are they sitting there tapping away at the controller while hallucinating these things happening (in which case, can they pause the game?), or do they stay in place, or even run around in circles, while pointing imaginary weapons at imaginary foes?

Oh, also, it's "anomalous".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written and interesting, at least to me. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, I'll fix that. The added log is mainly to respond to a suggestion about things that I didn't cover in the article (like if you tried to dump the code and such).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, dunno. The added tests don't show that much interesting properties, or at least not interesting to a super-tired me.

Also, "researcher using mechanical arm" ? Don't we have Class D personnel for that? I mean, with what it does, how did that ever get approved?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey everyone, I added a second Test Log that contains the results of various experiments on the game. It might add a little more to the SCP :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, those are good ideas, and I didn't think of them. I'll make a few additions to the article (and maybe another Test Log containing the results of dumping the ROM) later, though I tried to avoid talking about dumping the ROM because I wanted to maintain simplicity and not have the article sound too technical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll go through that Test Log again later and add a few more levels, and maybe expand upon the expunged ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, you're right, I never really covered that, did I? I'd like to imagine that it's all psychological, up until the point where they vanish, but I think you could easily draw either interpretation, whichever's scarier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was late at night when I wrote it, so I probably didn't put as much effort into the test log as I could have. I'll work on it a little more today, and add a few more levels, to build up some more tension before Level 33. Thanks for the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got it. I thought some might appreciate the reference. Aside from 093 and 087, it's my favourite SCP. The damn picture freaks me out every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Level 18. I wonder what SCP that could be? Also, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it were an RPG, would the guy start going into random people's houses and looking through their drawers and stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and at one point was thinking of doing a variation on it: A video game (or maybe an RPG) where if you play for a while, you start hallucinating that you are the hero (or villain) in the game world, and begin acting accordingly in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to put it out there that I love it when scps relating to old game stuff succeed. Upvoted gleefully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to post something snarky but this really grew on me when I read the test log. I agree that it peters out at the end though. We go from wild animals, to crazed killers, to shadow entities, to [REDACTED]? Horror is a big genre, man, you could have put in a lot more.

That said, I like this a lot. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Level 33

[DATA EXPUNGED]

Eeeeuuuurrrggghhh. I can't bring myself to like the core concept, so a downvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd imagine 1) Real-life weaponry works, 2) Visible to everyone. Because that makes it so much scarier that no amount of Foundation weaponry can protect the player from level 33.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. Some questions:

1) Can a player use a real-life weapon on the game opponents, or will it simply pass through them?

2) Can non-players see the damage done by the game to the players, or does it use some sort of hit-point system that's not physically reflected in their bodies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You didn't really cover what would happen if no humans were holding the controllers when the game was activated. An NES controller could easily be wired up to provide the appropriate commands using a timer with no human intervention.

You never really covered what happens when the console is turned back on while the game is being played after it was turned off prior

Given that it is an NES game, it should also be feasible to dump the ROM and look through all the hints the game gives to get a full listing of the levels. Since you probably wouldn't allow this to happen in the article, you could report what they found instead of this. The game being incredibly small, the actual code to start the game causes the machine to fault despite that the game continues to run, there is no code on the cartridge, etc. You have a lot of room to expand.

Also, an extension of the above idea, dumping the rom and putting it on a new cartridge or running it on an emulator. This would narrow down whether it is the cartridge or rom that is anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others have said, "games what kills you" is boring now. I do like this article, though. It is well written with decent tone. just a little more tightening up in the expungements. I found level 31 and 33 to be very anticlimactic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the basic idea. A virtual Jumanji on a particularly bad batch of PCP. What I'm wondering though is how players lose challenges. Do they actually get hurt or is it all psychological?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know, I have all 3. Good article man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm way ahead of you. I often write things more superfluously than I need to the first time, and I clean them up later when I re-read. I'll go through it soon when I have time and do a general cleanup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I feel like the language needs to be tightened up in a bunch of places… other than that, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There was also the European (PAL) version, which was also known as the NES. It was not the same as the North American one :)
There are 2 versions of the NES, plus the Japanese Famicom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is no north american version of the nes. in japan it was the famicom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, I can try again. I must admit, it *was* the old video game trope of creepypasta that inspired me to write this, because I got a little bored and decided to see if I could put a fresh spin on the old "game that kills you" concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is hard to give constructive feedback about. On the one hand, it's basically well-written, and I especially like the manual excerpt. You've captured the tone of those early Nintendo manuals pretty well.

On the other hand, this is one of the most overused tropes in creepypasta. So what we've got here is a decent take on a bad idea.

This is a no vote from me, but I suspect it won't do well. Try again with a different object?

Edit: Apparently I'm wrong. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty well-written in terms of execution, though could use tightening in some places (the Foundation wouldn't refer to what happens to the game's players as "increasingly horrific", I think). The major problem is in the concept itself, "game what kills you." I think we have at least one or two SCPs that are games-what-kill-you (that I don't love either, really). Moreover, as someone in chat pointed out, the prevalence of anomalous video games in creepypasta in general leaves me pretty inured to the overall horror of the situation.

No vote; it's not bad, but it doesn't blow me away, either.
